title: Blog

Replication of Karlan and List (2007)


A replication of key findings from Karlan and List (2007) on matching grants and charitable giving.



Jiyoung Koo


Apr 20, 2026











A/B Testing a Call to Action


A simulation-based introduction to A/B testing and key ideas in classical frequentist statistics.

title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
text:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import statsmodels.api as sm

plt.rcParams["figure.figsize"] = (8, 5)
plt.rcParams["axes.spines.top"] = False
plt.rcParams["axes.spines.right"] = False

title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
text:
Introduction
Karlan and List (2007) study whether matching grants affect charitable giving. In their field experiment, prior donors to a nonprofit organization were randomly assigned to receive either a standard fundraising letter or a letter that included a matching grant offer. The main question is whether lowering the effective “price” of giving through a match increases the likelihood that people donate.
This is an important causal question because fundraisers often use matching campaigns to increase donations, but it is not obvious whether these offers truly change behavior or simply repackage the same appeal. Because the authors use random assignment, differences in outcomes between the treatment and control groups can be interpreted causally.
In this post, I replicate three parts of their analysis. First, I check whether treatment and control groups look balanced on observable variables, which helps confirm that randomization worked. Second, I replicate the main finding that matching grants increase donation rates and average revenue per solicitation. Third, I estimate a regression similar to one of the specifications in Table 3. As an additional exercise, I also visualize the treatment effect graphically and explain whether the result mainly operates through the extensive or intensive margin.

title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
text:
Balance Check (Replicating Part of Table 1)
A key feature of a randomized experiment is that the treatment and control groups should be similar before treatment. If that is true, then differences in outcomes after the letters are sent are more credibly caused by the matching grant itself.
I examine a few observable variables that appear in Table 1:

freq: number of prior donations
HPA: highest previous amount donated
dormant: indicator for whether the donor had been inactive
ltmedMRA: indicator for whether the donor’s recent giving was below the median


balance_vars = ["freq", "HPA", "dormant", "ltmedMRA"]

balance_table = (
    df.groupby("treatment")[balance_vars]
      .mean()
      .T
      .rename(columns={0.0: "Control", 1.0: "Treatment"})
)

balance_table.round(3)







treatment
Control
Treatment




freq
8.047
8.035


HPA
58.960
59.597


dormant
0.523
0.524


ltmedMRA
0.488
0.497







The treatment and control groups are very similar on these observable characteristics. This supports the claim that randomization worked as intended and that there is no obvious evidence of selection bias between the two groups.

title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
text:
Main Analysis

Replicating Table 2A, Panel A
The core result in the paper is that matching grants increase both the response rate and the average amount raised per solicitation.


Response Rate

response_rate = df.groupby("treatment")["donate"].mean()
response_rate

treatment
0.0    0.017858
1.0    0.022039
Name: donate, dtype: float64



response_rate_percent = (100 * response_rate).round(2)
response_rate_percent

treatment
0.0    1.79
1.0    2.20
Name: donate, dtype: float64


The donation rate is about 1.79% in the control group and 2.20% in the treatment group. This means that offering a matching grant increases the probability of donating by roughly 23%.


Average Donation Amount (Unconditional)

avg_amount = df.groupby("treatment")["amount"].mean()
avg_amount

treatment
0.0    0.813268
1.0    0.966873
Name: amount, dtype: float32


The average amount raised per solicitation is about 0.81 in the control group and 0.97 in the treatment group. This is an increase of about 19%, which closely matches the main result in the paper.


Average Donation Conditional on Giving

conditional_amount = df[df["amount"] > 0].groupby("treatment")["amount"].mean()
conditional_amount

treatment
0.0    45.540268
1.0    43.871876
Name: amount, dtype: float32


Among those who donate, the average gift size is fairly similar across groups. This suggests that the matching grant mostly affects whether people choose to donate, rather than how much they give once they decide to contribute.

title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
text:
Regression Analysis
Because the assignment notes mention that Table 3 appears to have been produced using a standard linear regression even though the paper labels it as probit, I estimate a linear probability model using OLS.

temp = df[["donate", "treatment"]].copy().dropna()

X = sm.add_constant(temp["treatment"])
y = temp["donate"]

ols_model = sm.OLS(y, X)
ols_result = ols_model.fit()

print(ols_result.summary())

                            OLS Regression Results                            
==============================================================================
Dep. Variable:                 donate   R-squared:                       0.000
Model:                            OLS   Adj. R-squared:                  0.000
Method:                 Least Squares   F-statistic:                     9.618
Date:                Mon, 20 Apr 2026   Prob (F-statistic):            0.00193
Time:                        00:50:00   Log-Likelihood:                 26630.
No. Observations:               50083   AIC:                        -5.326e+04
Df Residuals:                   50081   BIC:                        -5.324e+04
Df Model:                           1                                         
Covariance Type:            nonrobust                                         
==============================================================================
                 coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
const          0.0179      0.001     16.225      0.000       0.016       0.020
treatment      0.0042      0.001      3.101      0.002       0.002       0.007
==============================================================================
Omnibus:                    59814.280   Durbin-Watson:                   1.997
Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):          4317152.727
Skew:                           6.740   Prob(JB):                         0.00
Kurtosis:                      46.440   Cond. No.                         3.23
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.


The coefficient on treatment is positive and statistically significant, indicating that receiving a matching offer increases the probability of donating. In a linear probability model, this coefficient can be interpreted directly as the change in donation probability associated with treatment.
This OLS specification is also useful because it aligns more closely with the assignment note that the Table 3 estimates appear to come from a standard linear regression.

Regression with Basic Controls
As a robustness check, I also add a few pre-treatment characteristics as controls.

controls = ["freq", "HPA", "dormant", "ltmedMRA"]
temp2 = df[["donate", "treatment"] + controls].copy().dropna()

X2 = sm.add_constant(temp2[["treatment"] + controls])
y2 = temp2["donate"]

ols_controls_model = sm.OLS(y2, X2)
ols_controls_result = ols_controls_model.fit()

print(ols_controls_result.summary())

                            OLS Regression Results                            
==============================================================================
Dep. Variable:                 donate   R-squared:                       0.016
Model:                            OLS   Adj. R-squared:                  0.016
Method:                 Least Squares   F-statistic:                     165.9
Date:                Mon, 20 Apr 2026   Prob (F-statistic):          1.45e-175
Time:                        00:50:00   Log-Likelihood:                 27036.
No. Observations:               50083   AIC:                        -5.406e+04
Df Residuals:                   50077   BIC:                        -5.401e+04
Df Model:                           5                                         
Covariance Type:            nonrobust                                         
==============================================================================
                 coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
const          0.0148      0.002      8.942      0.000       0.012       0.018
treatment      0.0042      0.001      3.128      0.002       0.002       0.007
freq           0.0013   5.74e-05     22.702      0.000       0.001       0.001
HPA         -1.49e-05    9.6e-06     -1.552      0.121   -3.37e-05    3.91e-06
dormant       -0.0163      0.001    -12.777      0.000      -0.019      -0.014
ltmedMRA       0.0041      0.001      3.045      0.002       0.001       0.007
==============================================================================
Omnibus:                    58989.422   Durbin-Watson:                   1.993
Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):          4091508.000
Skew:                           6.590   Prob(JB):                         0.00
Kurtosis:                      45.273   Cond. No.                         299.
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.


The treatment effect remains positive after adding controls, which supports the conclusion that the matching grant increased donation probability.
Because treatment was randomly assigned, the treatment coefficient can be interpreted causally: randomization helps ensure that other factors, such as prior donor characteristics or baseline giving tendencies, are balanced across groups on average.

title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
section: Something Additional: A Graphical Presentation of the Main Result
text:
Something Additional: A Graphical Presentation of the Main Result
As an additional exercise, I present the treatment effect graphically. The paper mainly reports this result in a table, but a simple figure makes the difference easy to see.

plot_df = (
    df.groupby("treatment")["donate"]
      .agg(["mean", "count", "std"])
      .reset_index()
)

plot_df["se"] = plot_df["std"] / np.sqrt(plot_df["count"])
plot_df["ci95"] = 1.96 * plot_df["se"]

labels = ["Control", "Treatment"]
means = plot_df["mean"]
errors = plot_df["ci95"]

plt.bar(labels, means, yerr=errors, capsize=6)
plt.ylabel("Response Rate")
plt.title("Effect of Matching Grant on Donation Probability")
plt.show()








The graph shows a clear increase in the response rate for the treatment group. The difference is small in absolute terms but meaningful relative to the low baseline donation rate.
This pattern also helps clarify the mechanism behind the main effect. The matching grant appears to operate mainly through the extensive margin: it gets more people to donate. By contrast, the conditional gift amount changes very little, so there is less evidence that the treatment strongly affects the intensive margin, or how much donors choose to give once they decide to donate.

title: Replication of Karlan and List (2007)
text:
Conclusion
This replication reproduces the main qualitative findings of Karlan and List (2007). First, the treatment and control groups appear balanced on observable characteristics, which supports the validity of the experimental design. Second, matching grants increase both the probability of donating and the average amount raised per solicitation. Third, an OLS regression confirms that the treatment effect is positive and statistically significant.
My additional graph also makes the main result easier to see and reinforces the interpretation that the effect works primarily by increasing participation rather than changing gift size among donors.
Overall, this paper provides a clear example of how randomized experiments can be used to draw causal conclusions in marketing and fundraising contexts.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Note



Write a short introduction for the reader that sets the scene. Cover the following points in your own words:

You have a website and your landing page includes a place where visitors can sign up to receive your newsletter.
You want to test which of two possible “calls to action” (CTAs) leads to more sign-ups. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” (you are welcome to change the language of the CTAs).
You will evaluate which CTA performs better by running an A/B test. Explain what an A/B test is: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and you compare sign-up rates between the two groups.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Note



Write a short introduction for the reader that sets the scene. Cover the following points in your own words:

You have a website and your landing page includes a place where visitors can sign up to receive your newsletter.
You want to test which of two possible “calls to action” (CTAs) leads to more sign-ups. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” (you are welcome to change the language of the CTAs).
You will evaluate which CTA performs better by running an A/B test. Explain what an A/B test is: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and you compare sign-up rates between the two groups.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem






Note



In this section, connect the A/B test to a formal statistical framework. Address each of the following ideas:

Each visitor either signs up or does not, so we can model each visitor’s outcome as a draw from a Bernoulli distribution with parameter \(\pi\), the probability of signing up.
Because the CTA a visitor sees may influence their probability of signing up, we allow the parameter to differ across groups: visitors who see CTA A have sign-up probability \(\pi_A\), and visitors who see CTA B have sign-up probability \(\pi_B\). Each visitor can either sign up (1) or not (0).
The quantity we care about is \(\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B\), the difference in sign-up rates between the two CTAs.
Our estimator of \(\theta\) is the difference in sample means, \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\). (Since each observation is either 0 or 1, the sample mean is simply the proportion of visitors who signed up — so \(\bar{X}_A = \hat\pi_A\) and \(\bar{X}_B = \hat\pi_B\).)

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data






Note




Write a brief paragraph for the reader explaining that in the real world we would not know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\), but for the purpose of this exercise we will set them ourselves so that we can study the behavior of our estimator when we know the truth.
Suppose \(\pi_A = 0.22\) and \(\pi_B = 0.18\), so the true difference is \(\theta = 0.04\).
Using a language of your choice, simulate 1,000 draws from each of the two Bernoulli distributions (2,000 total). Store the results so you can use them in the sections that follow.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers






Note




Write an introductory sentence or two explaining the Law of Large Numbers to the reader: as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the population mean. Connect this back to our estimator — the LLN is what gives us confidence that \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) will be close to the true \(\theta\) when our sample is large enough.
Then demonstrate this visually. Using the simulated data from the previous section, compute the element-wise differences between the CTA A draws and the CTA B draws to get a vector of 1,000 differences. Calculate the cumulative average of that vector (i.e., the running mean after 1 draw, after 2 draws, …, after 1,000 draws) and plot it against the number of observations.
The plot should show a noisy average when only a few observations are included that settles down and converges toward the true difference of 0.04 as the sample size grows.
After the plot, write a few sentences explaining what the reader should take away from it.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors






Note




Now that we “trust” our estimator, the next natural question is: how precise is it? We would like to report not just a point estimate of \(\theta\) but also a confidence interval that communicates our uncertainty.
Write a short introduction explaining the idea of bootstrapping to the reader. The bootstrap is a resampling method that lets us estimate the variability of a statistic without relying on a closed-form formula. We resample (with replacement) from our observed data many times, compute the statistic of interest on each resample, and use the standard deviation of those resampled statistics as our estimate of the standard error.
Then carry out the following steps:

From your 1,000 CTA A draws and 1,000 CTA B draws, draw a bootstrap resample of size 1,000 (with replacement) from each group. Compute \(\hat\theta^* = \bar{X}_A^* - \bar{X}_B^*\) for that resample.
Repeat step 1 a total of 1,000 times to produce a vector of 1,000 bootstrapped estimates of \(\theta\).
Compute the standard deviation of those 1,000 values. This is your bootstrapped standard error.
Compare it to the analytical standard error. For the difference in means of two independent Bernoulli samples, the standard error formula is:


\[
SE(\hat\theta) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat\pi_A(1 - \hat\pi_A)}{n_A} + \frac{\hat\pi_B(1 - \hat\pi_B)}{n_B}}
\]
where \(\hat\pi_A\) and \(\hat\pi_B\) are the sample proportions and \(n_A\) and \(n_B\) are the sample sizes.

Report both the bootstrapped standard error and the analytical standard error and comment on how close they are to each other. Then use the bootstrapped standard error to construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\theta\) and interpret it for the reader.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem






Note




Write a short introduction explaining the Central Limit Theorem to the reader: regardless of the shape of the underlying population distribution, the sampling distribution of the sample mean (and therefore of the difference in sample means) becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows.
Then demonstrate this visually by creating four histograms, one for each of the following sample sizes: \(n \in \{25, 50, 100, 500\}\).
For each sample size \(n\):

Draw \(n\) observations from the CTA A distribution and \(n\) observations from the CTA B distribution.
Compute \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).
Repeat steps 1–2 a total of 1,000 times to produce 1,000 values of \(\hat\theta\).
Plot a histogram of those 1,000 values.

Arrange the four histograms so they can be compared easily (e.g., in a 2x2 grid with consistent x-axis scales).
After the plots, write a few sentences explaining what the reader should observe: at small sample sizes the histogram may look rough or asymmetric, but as \(n\) increases the distribution of \(\hat\theta\) becomes smoother and more bell-shaped, illustrating the Central Limit Theorem in action.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing






Note




Now that we have seen the CLT in action — that the sampling distribution of \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples — we can use this result to perform a formal hypothesis test.
Write a paragraph explaining what a hypothesis test is to the reader. We set up a null hypothesis \(H_0: \theta = 0\) (i.e., the two CTAs produce the same sign-up rate) and an alternative hypothesis \(H_1: \theta \neq 0\). The goal is to assess whether the data provide enough evidence to reject \(H_0\).
Then explain why we standardize \(\hat\theta\) to form a test statistic. We compute:

\[
z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}
\]

Walk the reader through the logic of this standardization step by step:

By the CLT, we know that \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples. Under \(H_0\), the mean of that distribution is 0.
When we divide a Normal random variable by its standard deviation, we get a standard Normal \(N(0,1)\). So under \(H_0\), \(z \;\dot\sim\; N(0,1)\).
This means we know the distribution of our test statistic under the null, which is exactly what we need to compute a p-value.

Explain to the reader why this is sometimes called a “t-test” even though the test statistic follows an approximate Normal distribution, not a t-distribution. The classical t-test arises when the data are drawn from a Normal distribution and the variance must be estimated — the ratio then follows an exact t-distribution. In our setting the data are Bernoulli (not Normal), so there is no exact t-distribution result. The CLT gives us approximate Normality, and since we estimate the SE from the data, what we are really doing is closer to a z-test. In practice, for large samples the t-distribution and the standard Normal are nearly identical, so the distinction has little practical consequence — but it is important to understand why.
Using your simulated data, compute the test statistic and its associated p-value (using the standard Normal distribution as the reference). Interpret the result for the reader.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression






Note




It turns out that the two-sample test we just ran is mathematically equivalent to a simple linear regression. Understanding this connection is useful because it means the same machinery — and the same software — that you use for regression can also perform hypothesis tests, and it generalizes naturally to more complex experimental designs.
Explain the setup to the reader. Stack the CTA A and CTA B data into a single dataset with two columns: an outcome \(Y_i\) (1 if the visitor signed up, 0 otherwise) and an indicator \(D_i\) (1 if the visitor saw CTA A, 0 if they saw CTA B). Then fit the regression:

\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i
\]

Walk through the interpretation of the coefficients:

\(\beta_0\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 0\) — i.e., the mean of the B group, which estimates \(\pi_B\).
\(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 1\) — i.e., the mean of the A group, which estimates \(\pi_A\).
Therefore \(\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta\), and the OLS estimate \(\hat\beta_1\) is numerically identical to \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).

Fit this regression using your simulated data. Report the coefficient estimate \(\hat\beta_1\), its standard error, its t-statistic, and its p-value. Compare these to the values you computed in the previous section using the two-sample approach — they should match (up to a small difference in how the standard error is computed; standard OLS assumes a single pooled variance, while the two-sample formula we used assumes separate variances for each group).
Explain to the reader why this equivalence matters: the regression framework makes it easy to add controls, interactions, or additional treatment arms, all while producing the same inference in the simple case.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking






Note




Your boss is impatient. Rather than waiting until all 1,000 visitors have been assigned to each group, she wants to run the hypothesis test after every 100 visitors per group — peeking at the results after 100, 200, 300, …, up to 1,000 visitors per group (10 tests in total). If any of those tests comes back significant at the \(\alpha = 0.05\) level, she wants to declare a winner and stop the experiment early.
Explain to the reader why this is problematic under classical frequentist statistics. The key issue is that a single test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) has a 5% chance of a false positive (rejecting \(H_0\) when it is actually true). But when you conduct multiple tests on accumulating data, each peek is another opportunity to get a false positive. The overall probability of at least one false rejection across all the peeks is much higher than 5%.
Demonstrate this with a simulation. The idea is as follows:

Set up a world where the null hypothesis is true: \(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\) (i.e., \(\theta = 0\), there is no difference between the CTAs).
Simulate a single experiment: draw 1,000 observations for group A and 1,000 for group B. After every 100 observations per group (at \(n = 100, 200, \ldots, 1000\)), compute the z-statistic and p-value. Record whether any of the 10 p-values falls below 0.05.
Repeat step 2 a total of 10,000 times to simulate 10,000 experiments.
Compute the fraction of those 10,000 experiments in which at least one peek produced a significant result. This is the empirical false positive rate under peeking.

Compare this to the nominal 5% false positive rate of a single test. Report the result and explain to the reader why peeking inflates the Type I error rate and what this means in practice for running A/B tests.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import norm
import statsmodels.api as sm
import pandas as pd

np.random.seed(42)
plt.rcParams["figure.figsize"] = (8, 5)
plt.rcParams["axes.spines.top"] = False
plt.rcParams["axes.spines.right"] = False

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Introduction
Suppose I run a website with a landing page that invites visitors to subscribe to a newsletter. A small wording change in that invitation may affect whether people decide to sign up, so rather than relying on intuition, I want to test two alternative calls to action.
In this example, CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!”. To evaluate which version performs better, I run an A/B test. In an A/B test, visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two versions, and then I compare the sign-up rates across the groups. Because assignment is random, any systematic difference in outcomes can be attributed to the CTA rather than to differences in the visitors themselves.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
Each visitor either signs up or does not sign up, so each individual outcome can be modeled as a draw from a Bernoulli distribution with parameter \(\pi\), where \(\pi\) is the probability of signing up.
Because the language of the CTA may affect user behavior, I allow that probability to differ across the two groups. Visitors who see CTA A have sign-up probability \(\pi_A\), while visitors who see CTA B have sign-up probability \(\pi_B\). In both groups, each observation takes only two possible values: 1 if the visitor signs up and 0 if the visitor does not.
The quantity I care about is the difference in sign-up rates between the two versions:
\[
\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B
\]
To estimate this quantity, I use the difference in sample means:
\[
\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B
\]
Because each observation is either 0 or 1, the sample mean is just the proportion of visitors who signed up. That means \(\bar{X}_A = \hat\pi_A\) and \(\bar{X}_B = \hat\pi_B\), so the estimator is simply the observed difference in sign-up rates.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In a real A/B test, I would not know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\). For this exercise, however, it is useful to set those values ourselves so that we can study how the estimator behaves when we know the truth. This lets us compare what the simulation produces to the actual underlying treatment effect.
Here I assume that the true sign-up probability under CTA A is 0.22 and the true sign-up probability under CTA B is 0.18. That means the true treatment effect is:
\[
\theta = 0.22 - 0.18 = 0.04
\]
I now simulate 1,000 draws from each Bernoulli distribution.

n = 1000
pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18
theta_true = pi_A - pi_B

A = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n)
B = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n)

pi_A_hat = A.mean()
pi_B_hat = B.mean()
theta_hat = pi_A_hat - pi_B_hat

summary_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Group": ["CTA A", "CTA B"],
    "True sign-up rate": [pi_A, pi_B],
    "Sample sign-up rate": [pi_A_hat, pi_B_hat],
    "Sample size": [n, n]
})

summary_df.round(4)








Group
True sign-up rate
Sample sign-up rate
Sample size




0
CTA A
0.22
0.216
1000


1
CTA B
0.18
0.178
1000







The simulated sample proportions are not exactly equal to the true probabilities, but they should be reasonably close. That is exactly what we expect from random sampling.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers tells us that as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the population mean. In this setting, that idea is important because it helps justify why \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) should be close to the true \(\theta\) once the sample is large enough.
To illustrate this, I compute the element-wise differences between the simulated CTA A and CTA B outcomes, then calculate the cumulative average of that difference vector.

diff = A - B
cum_avg = np.cumsum(diff) / np.arange(1, n + 1)

plt.plot(np.arange(1, n + 1), cum_avg, linewidth=2)
plt.axhline(theta_true, linestyle="--", linewidth=2)
plt.xlabel("Number of observations")
plt.ylabel("Cumulative average of A - B")
plt.title("Law of Large Numbers: Running estimate of the treatment effect")
plt.show()








At the beginning of the sample, the running average moves around quite a bit because each additional observation has a large impact on the estimate. As more observations are added, the line becomes much more stable and gradually settles near the true value of 0.04.
The main lesson from this plot is that larger samples reduce noise. The estimator is still random, but with enough observations it becomes much more reliable. This is the practical value of the Law of Large Numbers in an A/B testing context.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
Once I have an estimate of the treatment effect, the next question is how precise that estimate is. A point estimate alone does not tell us how much uncertainty remains, so it is useful to report a standard error and a confidence interval.
One way to estimate the standard error is by using the bootstrap. The bootstrap is a resampling method that avoids relying entirely on a closed-form formula. The basic idea is to repeatedly resample from the observed data, with replacement, and recalculate the statistic of interest each time. The standard deviation of those resampled statistics provides an estimate of the standard error.
I now generate 1,000 bootstrap resamples. In each resample, I draw 1,000 observations with replacement from group A and 1,000 observations with replacement from group B, compute the difference in means, and store the result.

boot_thetas = []

for _ in range(1000):
    A_star = np.random.choice(A, size=n, replace=True)
    B_star = np.random.choice(B, size=n, replace=True)
    theta_star = A_star.mean() - B_star.mean()
    boot_thetas.append(theta_star)

boot_thetas = np.array(boot_thetas)
boot_se = boot_thetas.std(ddof=1)

analytic_se = np.sqrt(
    (pi_A_hat * (1 - pi_A_hat)) / n +
    (pi_B_hat * (1 - pi_B_hat)) / n
)

ci_lower = theta_hat - 1.96 * boot_se
ci_upper = theta_hat + 1.96 * boot_se

se_results = pd.DataFrame({
    "Quantity": [
        "Point estimate",
        "Bootstrap standard error",
        "Analytical standard error",
        "95% CI lower bound",
        "95% CI upper bound"
    ],
    "Value": [theta_hat, boot_se, analytic_se, ci_lower, ci_upper]
})

se_results.round(4)








Quantity
Value




0
Point estimate
0.0380


1
Bootstrap standard error
0.0177


2
Analytical standard error
0.0178


3
95% CI lower bound
0.0034


4
95% CI upper bound
0.0726







The bootstrap standard error and the analytical standard error should be very close to one another. That is reassuring, because it suggests that both methods are capturing the same underlying uncertainty in the estimate.
Using the bootstrap standard error, the 95% confidence interval for \(\theta\) is the point estimate plus or minus 1.96 standard errors. Interpreted in plain language, this interval gives a plausible range for the true difference in sign-up rates between the two CTAs. If the interval excludes zero, that suggests evidence of a real difference between the two versions.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem says that regardless of the shape of the underlying population distribution, the sampling distribution of the sample mean becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows. Since my estimator is the difference in two sample means, the same idea applies here as well.
To demonstrate this, I simulate the sampling distribution of \(\hat\theta\) for four different sample sizes: \(n \in \{25, 50, 100, 500\}\). For each sample size, I repeatedly draw observations from the two Bernoulli distributions, compute the difference in means, and repeat that process 1,000 times.

def simulate_theta_dist(sample_size, pi_A, pi_B, reps=1000):
    estimates = []
    for _ in range(reps):
        A_sim = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, sample_size)
        B_sim = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, sample_size)
        estimates.append(A_sim.mean() - B_sim.mean())
    return np.array(estimates)

sample_sizes = [25, 50, 100, 500]
theta_sims = {s: simulate_theta_dist(s, pi_A, pi_B, reps=1000) for s in sample_sizes}


fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(11, 8), sharex=True, sharey=True)
axes = axes.flatten()

all_vals = np.concatenate(list(theta_sims.values()))
xmin, xmax = all_vals.min(), all_vals.max()

for ax, s in zip(axes, sample_sizes):
    ax.hist(theta_sims[s], bins=30)
    ax.axvline(theta_true, linestyle="--", linewidth=2)
    ax.set_title(f"n = {s}")
    ax.set_xlim(xmin, xmax)
    ax.set_xlabel(r"$\hat\theta$")
    ax.set_ylabel("Frequency")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








At smaller sample sizes, the histograms look rougher and less smooth. As the sample size increases, the distribution becomes more regular and more bell-shaped. This is the Central Limit Theorem in action.
The important takeaway is that with large enough samples, the sampling distribution of the difference in means can be approximated well by a Normal distribution. That approximation is what makes large-sample hypothesis testing possible.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
Now that I have seen the Central Limit Theorem in action, I can use it to perform a formal hypothesis test. The purpose of a hypothesis test is to evaluate whether the observed difference in sign-up rates could reasonably have occurred if there were actually no difference between the two CTAs.
The null and alternative hypotheses are:
\[
H_0: \theta = 0
\]
\[
H_1: \theta \neq 0
\]
To test the null, I standardize the estimate using its standard error:
\[
z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}
\]
The logic behind this is straightforward. First, the CLT tells us that \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normally distributed in large samples. Second, under the null hypothesis that distribution is centered at 0. Third, when a Normal random variable is divided by its standard deviation, the result follows a standard Normal distribution. That means under \(H_0\), the statistic \(z\) is approximately distributed as \(N(0,1)\), which gives us a reference distribution for computing a p-value.
This is sometimes called a t-test, but in this setting it is more accurate to think of it as a large-sample z-test. The classical exact t-test comes from Normally distributed data with an estimated variance. Here the data are Bernoulli rather than Normal, so there is no exact t-distribution result. Instead, we rely on the CLT and a Normal approximation. In large samples, the practical difference is very small, but the conceptual distinction is still worth understanding.

z_stat = theta_hat / analytic_se
p_value = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z_stat)))

ht_results = pd.DataFrame({
    "Statistic": ["Estimate", "Standard error", "z-statistic", "p-value"],
    "Value": [theta_hat, analytic_se, z_stat, p_value]
})

ht_results.round(4)








Statistic
Value




0
Estimate
0.0380


1
Standard error
0.0178


2
z-statistic
2.1388


3
p-value
0.0325







A small p-value indicates that the observed difference would be unlikely under the null hypothesis of no treatment effect. In this simulation, because the true difference is 0.04, I would expect the test to often provide evidence against the null, although randomness still matters in any single sample.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
It turns out that this two-sample comparison is mathematically equivalent to a simple linear regression. That is useful because it shows that A/B testing can be analyzed using the same tools that are commonly used for regression, and those tools extend naturally to more complex settings.
To do this, I stack the two groups into a single dataset. Let \(Y_i\) be the outcome for visitor \(i\), equal to 1 if the visitor signs up and 0 otherwise. Let \(D_i\) be an indicator equal to 1 if the visitor saw CTA A and 0 if the visitor saw CTA B. I then estimate the regression:
\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i
\]
In this model, \(\beta_0\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 0\), which is the mean of group B. The quantity \(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 1\), which is the mean of group A. Therefore, \(\beta_1\) must be the difference in means, \(\pi_A - \pi_B = \theta\). That means the OLS estimate \(\hat\beta_1\) is numerically identical to \(\hat\theta\).

Y = np.concatenate([A, B])
D = np.concatenate([np.ones(n), np.zeros(n)])

reg_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Y": Y,
    "D": D
})

X = sm.add_constant(reg_df["D"])
model = sm.OLS(reg_df["Y"], X).fit()

reg_results = pd.DataFrame({
    "Statistic": ["Coefficient on D", "Standard error", "t-statistic", "p-value"],
    "Value": [
        model.params["D"],
        model.bse["D"],
        model.tvalues["D"],
        model.pvalues["D"]
    ]
})

reg_results.round(4)








Statistic
Value




0
Coefficient on D
0.0380


1
Standard error
0.0178


2
t-statistic
2.1377


3
p-value
0.0327








comparison = pd.DataFrame({
    "Approach": ["Two-sample test", "Regression"],
    "Estimate": [theta_hat, model.params["D"]],
    "SE": [analytic_se, model.bse["D"]],
    "Test statistic": [z_stat, model.tvalues["D"]],
    "p-value": [p_value, model.pvalues["D"]]
})

comparison.round(4)








Approach
Estimate
SE
Test statistic
p-value




0
Two-sample test
0.038
0.0178
2.1388
0.0325


1
Regression
0.038
0.0178
2.1377
0.0327







The coefficient estimate from the regression matches the difference-in-means estimate from the earlier section. The standard errors may differ slightly because the regression output uses the standard OLS variance formula, while the earlier analytical formula was tailored specifically to Bernoulli samples.
This equivalence matters because the regression framework makes it easy to add controls, interaction terms, or multiple treatments. In the simple two-group case, it gives the same basic answer, but it also opens the door to more flexible experimental analysis.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
Now suppose a manager is impatient and does not want to wait until the full sample has been collected. Instead, she checks the hypothesis test after every 100 visitors per group and stops the experiment early if any one of those tests is significant at the 5% level.
This is a problem under classical frequentist inference. A single test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) has a 5% chance of producing a false positive when the null is true. But when I repeatedly test the data as it accumulates, each peek creates another opportunity to reject the null by chance alone. The overall probability of at least one false rejection becomes much larger than 5%.
To show this, I simulate a world in which the null hypothesis is true, so that \(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\). In each simulated experiment, I generate 1,000 observations for each group and compute a p-value after every 100 observations per group. If any one of those p-values is below 0.05, I count that experiment as a false positive. I then repeat the entire process 10,000 times.

def run_peeking_experiment(n_total=1000, p=0.20, step=100, alpha=0.05):
    A_null = np.random.binomial(1, p, n_total)
    B_null = np.random.binomial(1, p, n_total)

    for i in range(step, n_total + 1, step):
        A_sub = A_null[:i]
        B_sub = B_null[:i]

        theta_i = A_sub.mean() - B_sub.mean()
        pA_i = A_sub.mean()
        pB_i = B_sub.mean()

        se_i = np.sqrt(
            (pA_i * (1 - pA_i)) / i +
            (pB_i * (1 - pB_i)) / i
        )

        if se_i == 0:
            continue

        z_i = theta_i / se_i
        pval_i = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z_i)))

        if pval_i < alpha:
            return 1

    return 0

n_experiments = 10000
peeking_results = np.array([run_peeking_experiment() for _ in range(n_experiments)])
false_positive_rate = peeking_results.mean()

peeking_summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Scenario": ["Nominal false positive rate from one test", "Empirical false positive rate with peeking"],
    "Rate": [0.05, false_positive_rate]
})

peeking_summary.round(4)








Scenario
Rate




0
Nominal false positive rate from one test
0.0500


1
Empirical false positive rate with peeking
0.1987







The simulated false positive rate under peeking is usually much higher than 0.05. That means repeated checking makes it much easier to incorrectly declare a winner even when the two CTAs are actually identical.
In practice, this matters because A/B tests are often monitored in real time. If the stopping rule is not planned in advance, ordinary p-values can become misleading. Classical frequentist inference assumes a fixed analysis plan, and peeking violates that assumption.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Conclusion
This A/B testing example illustrates several core ideas in classical frequentist statistics. The Law of Large Numbers shows why sample averages become more reliable as the sample grows. The bootstrap provides a practical way to measure uncertainty. The Central Limit Theorem explains why Normal approximations become useful for inference. Hypothesis testing gives a formal framework for evaluating evidence, and the regression formulation shows that the same logic can be expressed in a broader modeling language.
Just as importantly, the peeking example shows that sound inference depends not only on formulas but also on disciplined experimental practice. A/B testing is powerful, but only when it is paired with careful statistical reasoning.

title: Hi, I’m Jiyoung.
text:
Hi, I’m Jiyoung.
I am a data-driven Finance & Strategy professional and CPA, currently pursuing an M.S. in Business Analytics at UC San Diego.
I am passionate about leveraging data to improve financial performance, optimize operations, and support strategic decision-making.
Outside of work, I enjoy swing dancing, tennis, running, and golf.
